title: Making videos should be part of the evaluation process in school, not just writting

transcript.text: Hello. Part of the evaluation process in universities and high schools, in grade schools, should be, um, should be videos, talking in videos, not just tests, like if you do the test on, um, the history of America, you take the multiple choice test, but also they should do a video, because a lot of kids are getting accused of plagiarizing, especially nowadays,

Because you can just cut and paste from random places, and maybe there's even, like, underground websites where you can, like, buy people's papers and stuff so that the teacher would never have read something that was published, and, um, and, uh, I've known people and heard of people who were accused of plagiarizing when they didn't do it, and, uh, but having a video as part of it could be a good way for the teacher to not think that the person was plagiarizing,

Because they see the person right there talking about it, proving that they know the thing, um, and, uh, talking is, is a good way to help you learn, also. Um, I learn stuff from making videos because I make the video, and while making the video, I learn, because I'm talking and remembering and vocalizing what I remember, so it's activating my brain that way.

But then I watch the videos again, and, uh, and then I'm always, when I'm watching them, I'm like, I'm kind of loopy, like, I'd be talking about something, and I'd just use the wrong word, like, I'd be like, yeah, and then the Romans invaded Germany, but the Germans killed all the Romans, and so there were German bones everywhere.

It's like, well, there were Roman bones everywhere. I do that all the time. Um, so I try to, uh, but now, since I know that I do that, I'm gonna try and take my time to, uh, make sure that I get the words that are coming out of my mouth are the right ones, so.

And also, I remember stuff that I forgot, and I'm like, oh, you forgot about that. And so it's a good way to remember, um, to remember what you learn, and fortify what you memorize, and re-memorize, and, um, part of the learning process is talking and making videos.

Um, and, um, analyzing it doesn't really take that long for teachers. You just watch it once, and, uh, so you can get a lot more information across in a ten minute video, um, in ten minutes than you could writing.
Like, writing, writing the same amount of stuff that you would say in a video would take, like, ten times longer than just pushing record, talking, and then uploading it.
It's done. You know, no typing, no proofreading, no, and then reading, it's, watching a video, somebody talk, all that stuff, it takes a lot less time than, than reading them, do it.

So, you know, you could cover a lot more information. You could have people do the valuation with people, do videos, and that's all you do, just the videos, and then you just skip the tests and the papers.
All together. And then you, you know, so you can use the limited amount of time you have for the semester and the classes to, um, cover more area.
More subjects, because there's always more subjects to do.
Um, and everybody can have a video camera these days. They're really cheap. Everybody's got a video camera.
Hell, you've got a video, a nice high-res video camera on your cell phone.
So just do it with your cell phone, then you can upload it to the internet with your cell phone.
So, technology is here. If we don't use it, we're retarded.
Gotta use it, gotta use it.
